preFix.title: Patching an Artex or textured ceiling
preFix.subtitle: Fill and retexture a damaged area of a textured ceiling so the repair blends
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a damaged patch on a textured or Artex ceiling. Covers preparing the surface, matching the texture pattern, and painting to blend the repair into the surrounding area.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Artex applied before 1984 may contain chrysotile asbestos fibres. If the ceiling was textured before 1984 and has not been re-coated since, test it with a DIY asbestos kit or have it surveyed first. If asbestos is confirmed, this repair is not suitable: the work must be done by a licensed contractor.
A cracked or knocked-off patch of
Artex

stands out sharply against a textured background because flat filler has no pattern. The repair has two parts: filling the damage and recreating the texture. Neither is difficult, the hard part is reading the pattern before you start mixing.
Step 1, Check and prepare the surface

Knock lightly around the damaged area. Any hollow sound means the old coating is detached from the plaster behind and needs lifting before you fill. Scrape off loose texture back to a firm edge, always keeping the scraper at a low angle so the surrounding texture is not disturbed. Dust off and prime bare plaster with diluted PVA (1 part PVA to 5 parts water) before filling.
Step 2, Read the texture pattern
Look at the surrounding ceiling under a raking light, hold a torch at a low angle. Note whether the pattern is a random
stipple

, a swirl, a bark (long drags with a wide brush), or a fine comb pattern. Most domestic ceilings have stipple or swirl. Match the tool to the pattern: a balled-up piece of plastic carrier bag makes convincing stipple; a damp sponge pulled across the surface makes a coarser version; a wide brush twisted in arcs makes swirl.
Step 3, Fill the damage level

Mix lightweight one-coat filler to the consistency of thick porridge. Press into the damage with the 50 mm filling knife, working the filler into any cracks first. Draw the 100 mm knife across the repair to level it just below the existing texture surface, leave it about 1 to 2 mm low, not flush. The texture coat will bring it up to level. Wait for the filler to dry fully (typically 2 to 4 hours in a warm room; a full day in winter). Sand lightly with 120-grit paper wrapped around the sanding block, then wipe off the dust.
Step 4, Apply the texture coat

Ready-mixed texture coating is sold under various brand names (Artex Ready Mixed, Leyland Ceiling Texture, and others). Spread a thin even coat over the repair with the 100 mm filling knife, slightly overlapping the existing texture at the edges to feather the joint. While the coating is still wet, apply the texture using whatever tool matches the surrounding pattern. Work quickly, most texture coatings begin to skin after 10 to 15 minutes. Practise the pattern on a piece of board or cardboard before committing to the ceiling.
Step 5, Paint

Once the texture has dried overnight, prime the repair with a single coat of white emulsion thinned 10% with water (a mist coat). This seals the bare texture and stops it pulling unevenly when the finish coat goes on. Apply the mist coat with a brush, a roller pulls the fresh texture. When dry, apply one or two full coats of ceiling white with the roller. The roller should be medium or long pile; short pile doesn't reach into a textured surface. Feather the paint out well beyond the repair so there is no line.
Variations

Large patch: if the damage covers more than a quarter of the ceiling, consider removing all the texture by scraping and skim-coating the whole ceiling. A patchy ceiling with mismatched texture is harder to live with than a smooth one.
Lining paper as a repair: a coat of lining paper over the damaged area smooths over a large section uniformly. Paper the whole ceiling or at least the whole bay for the best result.
Troubleshooter

items.fix: Sand lightly, apply another thin texture coat, and re-pattern.
items.cause: Texture coat applied too thinly or worked too smooth
items.symptom: Repair shows as a flat patch after painting

items.fix: Apply a second finish coat once the first is dry. Prime fresh repairs before topcoating.
items.cause: Mist coat skipped, so the fresh compound absorbed the finish coat
items.symptom: Paint sinks into the texture unevenly

items.fix: Cut the crack out to a V-profile with a scraper, fill with flexible decorator's filler, leave to cure, then texture and paint.
items.cause: Movement crack, not a surface crack, the plaster itself is moving

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
